english: troublesome, difficult, hard, tsegs-čé (tshegs che) very troublesome, rkaṅ tsegs-čé (rkang tshegs che) Mil. much (fruitless) running to and fro; tsegs-méd (tshegs med) it is not difficult; tsegs-méd(-par) (tshegs med(-par)) easily adv.; tségs-pa (tshegs pa) trouble, toil, difficulty Sch.; pran - tsegs (phran tshegs) little troubles or difficulties Cs.

seam, cf. °tsém-pa ('tshem pa); tsém-bzo-pa (tshem bzo pa), tsém-pa (tshem pa) tailor W.; tsém-po °grol (tshem po 'grol) the seam opens, comes loose; tsem-méd (tshem med) without a seam; tsém-bu (tshem bu) Lex., Sch.: what has been stitched, darned, quilted.
resp. tooth, tséms-šiṅ (tshems shing) tooth-pick Dzl.
to have the disadvantage, to come off a loser, not receiving a full share Sch.
1. = tsar (tshar) time vulgo; tser - tsér (tsher tsher), Mil., prob. many times, repeatedly. — 2. v. the following.
W. also tse-ré (tshe re), tse-rí (tshe ri) sorrow, grief, pain, affliction, *tse-ré čo mi go* (*tshe re co mi go*) do not grieve! *tser čug-če* (*tsher bcug ce*) to afflict, to grieve (not in B.).

W. *tser-máṅ* (*tsher mang*) 1. thorn, prick, brier, Dzl. tser zug soṅ (tsher zug song) I have run a thorn into (my hand, foot); tser-mai mgo (tsher ma'i mgo) a deer's head po. spoken of Mil.; tsér-ma °dón-pa (tsher ma 'don pa) to pull out a thorn; nya-tsér (nya tsher) fish-bone Sch.; tsér-ma-čaṅ (tsher ma can) 1. thorny, prickly, briery. 2. like thorns, Thgy. — 2. thorn-bush, bramble, brake tser-dkár (tsher dkar), tser-stár (tsher star), buckthorn, Hippophaë rhamnoïdes, *tser-tar-lú-lu* (*tsher tar lu lu*) Ld., the berries of it (extremely sour). — tser-tágs (tsher thags) thorn-hedge (in Tibet gen. dead hedges). — tser-lúm (tsher lum) yellow raspberry Sik. tser-lhág (tsher lhag) n. of a disease Lt.

1. day of the month, tses-gráṅs (tshes grangs) date, always expressed by the cardinal number, tses-gčíg (tshes gcig) etc., tses-bču (tshes bcu) the tenth, in certain months a festival day, tses-bču-mčód-pa (tshes bcu mchod pa) sacrifice and beer-drinking on that day; tses-bčui °čám-yig (tshes bcu'i 'cham yig) programme of the religious dances performed on that occasion; zlá-ba tsés-pa (zla ba tshes pa) and tses-gsum-zlá-ba (tshes gsum zla ba). — 2. symb. num.: 15.

1. num. figure: 138. — 2. sbst. troop, number, host, yet hardly ever standing alone, or governing a genit. case, but like a termination affixed: gróṅ-mi-tso (grong mi tsho) the peasants (of the village), kyéd rnál-°byor -pa-tso (khyed rnal 'byor pa tsho) ye saints! In some instances its substantive character is more apparent, thus in tsóṅ-pa-tso (tshong pa tsho), mkás-pa-tso (mkhas pa tsho), bá-tso (ba tsho) it may be rendered by: a troop of merchants, a society of learned men (or the learned), a herd of cows (Cs.); but most frq. it stands (at least in later lit.) as plural termination of pronouns, so: ṅéd-tso (nged tsho) we, kóṅ-tso (khong tsho) they, °dí-tso ('di tsho) these, or it is affixed to numerals: °búm-tso ('bum tsho) 100 000. — yul-tso (yul tsho) v. yul (yul). — 3. adj. hot Bal.

fat, greasy, tso-kú (tsho khu) fat gravy, tso - ldír (tsho ldir) unwieldy with fatness (tso °dug mi °dug (tsho 'dug mi 'dug), or bud ma bud (bud ma bud), is it fat or not? being with young or not? Sch.?)

(cf. °tsógs-pa ('tshogs pa)) 1. an assemblage of men (implying, however, compared with tso (tsho), a larger number of individuals, not at once to be surveyed), Cs.: tsogs sdú-ba (tshogs sdu ba) to call an assembly, °gyéd-pa ('gyed pa) to dismiss it; tsogs °du (tshogs 'du) an assembly meets, °gye ('gye) it dissolves; W.: *šol soṅ* (*shol song*) it is adjourned, *tol soṅ* (*thol song*) it is broken up; dpuṅ(-gi)-tsogs (dpung(-gi)-tshogs) army frq.; yul-tsogs (yul tshogs) village community, country-parish, *yul-tsog nyi laṅ-te yod* (*yul tshog nyi lang te yod*) W. two parishes have set out; human society, tsógs-kyi náṅ-nas °byúṅ-ba (tshogs kyi nang nas 'byung ba) Sty., *tsog dhaṅ gyé-wa* (*tshog dang gye wa*) C. to retire from society; tsógs-naṅ mi °gró-ba (tshogs nang mi 'gro ba) not mixing with society Dó.; čós - tsogs (chos tshogs) has been introduced by us, with the concurrence of our native Christians, as the word for ‘congregation, church, ἐκκλησία’. — 2. accumulation, multitude, of things, *šiṅ-tsog* (*shing tshog*) W. wood, thicket, copse, bush, shrub; mé-tsogs (me tshogs) mass of fire, Thgy.; in a more special sense = dgé-bai tsogs (dge ba'i tshogs), or bsód-nams-kyi tsogs (bsod nams kyi tshogs), accumulation of merit acquired by virtue, tsogs bság-pa (tshogs bsag pa) to accumulate such frq.; tsogs ma bság-pai mi (tshogs ma bsag pa'i mi) almost the same as a wicked, godless person; tsogs(-kyi) °kór(-lo) (tshogs(-kyi) 'khor(-lo)), गणचक्र, sacrificial offering, a quantity of victuals, trinkets, and other articles being disposed in
